section.title: Israel's Kings to Ahab
section.sectionRationale: Chapter 15 traced Judah's kings under Asa. Chapter 16 turns north and rushes through four kings of Israel in quick, violent succession, Baasha judged and swept away, Elah murdered while drunk, Zimri's seven day reign ending in self-immolation, and Omri's rise out of civil war, before settling on Ahab, whose marriage to Jezebel and open enthronement of Baal in Samaria itself makes him, the narrator says plainly, more evil than all who were before him.
section.thesis: In one generation the northern kingdom devours four kings through assassination and civil war, and each new king walks further from the LORD than the last, until Ahab, marrying Jezebel and installing Baal worship at the heart of national life, becomes the worst king Israel has yet had, provoking the LORD to anger more than all before him and setting the stage for Elijah's contest for the nation's worship.
section.frameNote: Four beats: judgment pronounced and fulfilled on Baasha's house, verses 1 to 7; Elah's short reign and Zimri's seven day usurpation, verses 8 to 20; Omri's rise and the founding of Samaria, verses 21 to 28; and Ahab's marriage to Jezebel and installation of Baal worship, verses 29 to 34.

units.label: Judgment on Baasha's house
units.note: the word of the LORD against a doomed dynasty

verses.text: Then the {{dabar|word}} of the LORD came to Jehu son of Hanani against Baasha, saying:

“Even though I lifted you out of the dust and made you ruler over My people Israel, you have walked in the way of Jeroboam and have caused My people Israel to sin and to provoke Me to anger by their sins.
So now I will consume Baasha and his house, and I will make your house like that of Jeroboam son of Nebat:
Anyone belonging to Baasha who dies in the city will be eaten by dogs, and anyone who dies in the field will be eaten by the birds of the air.”
As for the rest of the acts of Baasha, along with his accomplishments and might, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel?
And Baasha rested with his fathers and was buried in Tirzah, and his son Elah reigned in his place.

Moreover, the word of the LORD came through the prophet Jehu son of Hanani against Baasha and his house, because of all the evil he had done in the sight of the LORD, provoking Him to anger with the work of his hands and becoming like the house of Jeroboam, and also because Baasha had struck down the house of Jeroboam.

units.label: Elah, Zimri, and a kingdom in chaos

verses.text: In the twenty-sixth year of Asa's reign over Judah, Elah son of Baasha became king of Israel, and he reigned in Tirzah two years.
However, while Elah was in Tirzah getting drunk in the house of Arza the steward of his household there, Elah's servant Zimri, the commander of half his chariots, conspired against him.
So in the twenty-seventh year of Asa's reign over Judah, Zimri went in, struck Elah down, and killed him. And Zimri reigned in his place.
As soon as Zimri began to reign and was seated on the throne, he struck down the entire household of Baasha. He did not leave a single male, whether a kinsman or friend.
So Zimri destroyed the entire household of Baasha, according to the word that the LORD had spoken against Baasha through Jehu the prophet.
This happened because of all the sins Baasha and his son Elah had committed and had caused Israel to commit, provoking the LORD, the God of Israel, to anger with their worthless idols.
As for the rest of the acts of Elah, along with all his accomplishments, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel?
In the twenty-seventh year of Asa's reign over Judah, Zimri reigned in Tirzah for seven days. Now the troops were encamped against Gibbethon of the Philistines,

and the people in the camp heard that Zimri had conspired against the king and struck him down. So there in the camp that very day, all Israel proclaimed Omri, the commander of the army, king over Israel.
Then Omri and all the Israelites marched up from Gibbethon and besieged Tirzah.
When Zimri saw that the city was captured, he entered the citadel of the royal palace and burned it down upon himself. So he died
because of the sins he had committed, doing evil in the sight of the LORD and following the example of Jeroboam and the sin he had committed and had caused Israel to commit.
As for the rest of the acts of Zimri and the treason he committed, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel?

units.label: Omri and the rise of Samaria
units.note: a divided kingdom, then a new capital

verses.text: At that time the people of Israel were divided: Half of the people supported Tibni son of Ginath as king, and half supported Omri.
But the followers of Omri proved stronger than those of Tibni son of Ginath. So Tibni died and Omri became king.
In the thirty-first year of Asa's reign over Judah, Omri became king of Israel, and he reigned twelve years, six of them in Tirzah.
He bought the hill of Samaria from Shemer for two talents of silver and built a city there, calling it Samaria after the name of Shemer, who had owned the hill.
But Omri did evil in the sight of the LORD and acted more wickedly than all who were before him.
For he walked in all the ways of Jeroboam son of Nebat and in his sins, which he caused Israel to commit, provoking the LORD, the God of Israel, to anger with their worthless idols.
As for the rest of the acts of Omri, along with his accomplishments and the might he exercised, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel?
And Omri rested with his fathers and was buried in Samaria, and his son Ahab reigned in his place.

units.label: Ahab: worse than all before him

verses.text: In the thirty-eighth year of Asa's reign over Judah, Ahab son of Omri became king of Israel, and he reigned in Samaria twenty-two years.
However, Ahab son of Omri did {{ra|evil}} in the sight of the LORD, more than all who were before him.

And as if it were not enough for him to walk in the sins of Jeroboam son of Nebat, he even married Jezebel the daughter of Ethbaal king of the Sidonians, and he then proceeded to serve and worship {{baal|Baal}}.
First, Ahab set up an altar for Baal in the temple of Baal that he had built in Samaria.
Then he set up an {{asherah|Asherah}} pole. Thus Ahab did more to provoke the LORD, the God of Israel, to anger than all the kings of Israel before him.

In Ahab's days, Hiel the Bethelite rebuilt Jericho. At the cost of Abiram his firstborn he laid its foundation, and at the cost of Segub his youngest he set up its gates, according to the word that the LORD had spoken through Joshua son of Nun.

dabar.gloss: Word, matter, thing, the spoken word of the LORD delivered through His prophets, understood in Israel as effectual and certain to be fulfilled. Chapter 16 opens with the dabar of the LORD condemning Baasha's house through Jehu, and closes with the dabar concerning Jericho, spoken generations earlier through Joshua, coming to pass exactly as declared, framing the whole chapter as proof that every word from the LORD stands.

ra.gloss: Evil, wickedness, what is bad or displeasing to the LORD. The verdict 'did evil in the sight of the LORD' tolls over Baasha, Elah, Zimri, and Omri in turn, but Ahab surpasses them all, so that the narrator's refrain builds to its darkest point in verse 30.

baal.gloss: Baal, meaning lord or master, the Canaanite storm and fertility god whose worship Jeroboam had tolerated at the edges but Ahab now installs at the center of national life, building him a temple and an altar in Samaria itself. The Elijah cycle that follows is a contest for whether the LORD or Baal is truly master.

asherah.gloss: Asherah, a wooden cult pole or the Canaanite goddess it represented, typically set up beside an altar as a symbol of fertility worship. Ahab's Asherah pole beside his Baal temple completes his adoption of the full Canaanite cult, provoking the LORD to anger more than any king before him.

basic.context: Chapter 15 followed Judah's kings; chapter 16 turns to the northern kingdom and rushes through four kings in a single generation. A prophet condemns Baasha's house for repeating Jeroboam's sins, and it is destroyed exactly as foretold when his son Elah is murdered by Zimri, one of his own officers. Zimri lasts only seven days before Omri's forces corner him and he burns the palace down on himself. Omri outlasts a rival and founds a new capital, Samaria, but he too does evil. Then his son Ahab surpasses them all, marrying the Sidonian princess Jezebel and building her god Baal a temple in Israel's own capital. The chapter raises the question of why Israel's kingship keeps collapsing into violence, and why each new king seems worse than the last.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative reporting Israel's political and spiritual decline; it is not a manual for statecraft or a promise that every coup is divine judgment in the same way. What the chapter does teach as an enduring truth is that the LORD's word about sin and consequence is not idle: it is spoken and it comes to pass, both against Baasha's house within the chapter and, startlingly, against Jericho generations after Joshua first spoke it, verse 34.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter is built on the refrain that each king did ra, evil, in the sight of the LORD, walking in the way of Jeroboam, until it climaxes with Ahab, who exceeds them by marrying into Baal worship and installing an altar and an Asherah pole in Samaria. The recurring dabar, word, of the LORD frames the whole chapter, spoken against Baasha at the start and fulfilled concerning Jericho at the close.
basic.typology: Israel's kings devour one another through murder and civil war within a single generation, unable to give the nation any lasting stability. This bleak parade of unstable rule sharpens the later promise of a King whose throne will never be shaken and whose government will have no end, fulfilled in Christ.
basic.christ: None of these kings can save Israel; each accelerates its ruin. This is exactly what makes the promise of an eternal, uncontested throne so striking centuries later: the LORD God will give Him the throne of His father David, and of His kingdom there will be no end, Luke 1:32-33. Where Israel's throne changes hands by murder four times in one generation, Christ's throne cannot be shaken. And the sure fulfillment of the LORD's ancient word over Jericho anticipates Him who is the Word made flesh, whose words will never pass away, John 1:14, Matthew 24:35.
basic.god: God is sovereign over the rise and fall of kings, even when they come to power through human violence and ambition. He is patient across four evil reigns, yet He is not indifferent: His word of judgment against Baasha's house is fulfilled precisely, and His ancient curse on Jericho's rebuilding falls due exactly on schedule. He is genuinely provoked to anger by covenant unfaithfulness, and His patience with Israel's kings is about to run its course.
basic.crossReferences: Joshua 6:26 records the curse on rebuilding Jericho, fulfilled here in verse 34. Deuteronomy 17:14-20 warns Israel's kings against foreign wives who would turn their hearts away, exactly what happens with Ahab and Jezebel. Hosea 8:4 later summarizes this whole era, they set up kings, but not by Me. And Luke 1:32-33 promises the eternal throne that contrasts with Israel's unstable succession here.
basic.oneStory: Israel spirals through a rapid, bloody succession of kings, each carrying forward Jeroboam's sin, until Ahab crowns the pattern by enthroning Baal himself in Samaria. This chapter shows human kingship at its worst and sets up the great contest of the following chapters, where the LORD will not share His people's worship with a rival. It is one more dark turn in the long story that will not find its resolution until the true King, of David's line, comes to reign forever.

context.literary: The chapter is structured as a rapid succession narrative, each king introduced, judged with a repeated formula, doing evil in the way of Jeroboam, and then dismissed with the standard notice of his acts and burial, until the pace and violence escalate: Baasha's house destroyed, Elah assassinated, Zimri's seven day reign ending in fiery suicide, Omri's civil war, and finally Ahab, whose evil is marked as categorically worse and given the most narrative space of any king in the chapter.
context.historical: The northern kingdom had no fixed dynastic succession like Judah's Davidic line, so it experienced repeated coups; Tirzah served as an early capital before Omri purchased the hill of Samaria and built a new one, a city that would remain Israel's capital until the Assyrian conquest. Ahab's marriage to Jezebel, daughter of the Sidonian king Ethbaal, was a political alliance with Phoenicia that imported the cult of Baal, the storm and fertility god, directly into Israel's court and capital.
context.canonical: This chapter closes the cycle of northern usurpers that began with Jeroboam and sets the stage for the Elijah narratives that dominate the rest of 1 Kings, in which the LORD contends publicly for Israel's exclusive worship against the Baal cult Ahab has now installed. Hiel's rebuilding of Jericho, verse 34, deliberately recalls Joshua 6:26, tying this chapter of decline back to the conquest era and forward to the coming contest on Carmel.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To trace how Israel's kingship collapsed into violent instability in a single generation, and to show that each king's evil, worsening from one to the next, culminates in Ahab, whose marriage to Jezebel and installation of Baal worship in Samaria makes the coming confrontation with Elijah necessary.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific, violent period of Israel's history; it does not prescribe political rebellion or coups as legitimate tools of judgment. What endures is the pattern it exposes, sin normalized across generations until it reaches a breaking point, and the sobering truth that the LORD's spoken word, whether of judgment or ancient curse, is never idle.

debates.question: Why does the narrator repeat the same judgment formula, did evil in the sight of the LORD, walking in the way of Jeroboam, for each king rather than assessing them individually?

views.label: To expose a systemic, inherited corruption
views.case: Jeroboam's golden calves had structurally corrupted Israel's worship from the outset, chapter 12; the repeated formula shows that each king simply inherited and perpetuated a corrupted system rather than making fresh individual choices, and none broke the cycle.

views.label: To build deliberate literary escalation toward Ahab
views.case: The near-identical refrain across four kings creates a rhythm the reader expects to continue, only for the formula to break open in verse 30 with more than all who were before him, sharpening the shock of just how much worse Ahab is.

debates.bearing: Either reading magnifies the same point: sin left unaddressed compounds across generations, and Ahab's unprecedented evil is the direct, escalating fruit of decades of unchecked idolatry.

debates.question: Is the violence of these coups, especially Zimri's total slaughter of Baasha's house, itself an act of divine judgment or simply human ambition?

views.label: Human ambition that the LORD sovereignly uses to fulfill His word
views.case: Zimri's motives are never described as righteous; he acts out of pure political self-interest, yet the narrator explicitly ties the outcome to the LORD's earlier prophecy against Baasha, verse 12, showing God's sovereign hand working through, not excusing, human violence.

views.label: Straightforward political history with a moral verdict attached
views.case: The text simply reports what happened and adds the theological verdict that Baasha's sins caused this outcome, without implying Zimri himself was righteous or commissioned; the fulfillment of prophecy and the wickedness of the instrument can be stated side by side.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm that the LORD's sovereignty over history does not require approving the methods of ambitious, violent men; He remains just even when working out judgment through fallen human choices.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's spine is the recurring verdict that each king did ra, evil, in the sight of the LORD, walking in the way of Jeroboam son of Nebat, a refrain that tightens with each king until it snaps open at Ahab, who did evil more than all who were before him. Ahab's evil is then given content: he serves and worships baal, Baal, the Canaanite lord of storm and fertility, going so far as to build him a temple and altar in Samaria and to raise an asherah pole beside it. And framing the whole chapter is the dabar, the word, of the LORD, spoken against Baasha at the start through the prophet Jehu and fulfilled precisely, and spoken generations earlier by Joshua against anyone who would rebuild Jericho, fulfilled with startling cost at the chapter's close.

typology.type: A kingdom devouring its own kings through repeated coups and civil war
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true King whose throne is established forever and cannot be seized or overthrown, Luke 1:32-33, 2 Samuel 7:16.

typology.type: The LORD's ancient word over Jericho, spoken by Joshua, coming to pass exactly centuries later
typology.fulfillment: The certainty of every word Christ has spoken, none of which will pass away, Matthew 24:35, and His own identity as the eternal Word, John 1:1, 14.

typology.type: A foreign wife drawing the king's heart after another god
typology.fulfillment: The pure, undivided devotion Christ, the true Bridegroom, both requires and grants to His bride, the Church, 2 Corinthians 11:2, Ephesians 5:25-27.

study.christ: None of these kings, gained by murder or civil war, can rescue Israel; each one deepens the nation's ruin, climaxing in Ahab's open embrace of Baal. The chapter's bleak parade of unstable, violent rule is exactly what gives urgency to the later promise of a very different King: of the increase of His government and peace there will be no end, and the LORD God will give Him the throne of His father David, Isaiah 9:7, Luke 1:32-33. Where Israel's historical kings devour one another, Christ's throne is established forever. And the precision with which the LORD's word about Jericho falls into place after so many generations, verse 34, anticipates Him who is the Word made flesh, whose every word likewise cannot fail, John 1:14, Matthew 24:35.
study.god: God is sovereign over the rise and fall of every king, working His purposes even through the violent ambitions of men like Zimri and Omri without becoming the author of their sin. He is patient, allowing four evil reigns to run their course before the great confrontation of the following chapters, yet He is not passive: His spoken word of judgment against Baasha's house is fulfilled to the letter, and His far older word against Jericho's rebuilder is honored generations later at great cost to Hiel's own family. He is genuinely provoked to anger by covenant unfaithfulness, and Ahab's marriage and idolatry mark the moment His patience is about to give way to open contest.

crossReferences.note: Baasha's own earlier coup against Jeroboam's house, the very sin for which Baasha himself is now judged and destroyed in the same manner.
The ancient curse on anyone who would rebuild Jericho, fulfilled exactly in verse 34 through the deaths of Hiel's sons.
The law of kingship warning that a king's foreign wives would turn his heart away, precisely what happens through Ahab's marriage to Jezebel.
They set up kings, but not by Me, a later prophetic summary of exactly this era of Israel's self-made, unstable kingship.
The angel's promise of an eternal, unshakable throne for Christ, standing in sharp contrast to the four kings devoured within this single chapter.

study.oneStory: Israel spirals through a rapid, bloody succession of kings, each one carrying forward Jeroboam's sin a little further, until Ahab crowns the pattern by marrying Jezebel and enthroning Baal in Samaria itself. This chapter shows human kingship at its very worst and sets up the great contest of the chapters that follow, where the LORD will not let His name be shared with a rival. It is one more dark turn in the long story of God's people, a story that will not find its resolution until the true King, of David's line, comes to reign forever.

quiz.q: What pattern connects Baasha, Elah, Zimri, and Omri as kings of Israel?

options.text: Each did evil in the sight of the LORD, walking in the way of Jeroboam, and each dynasty was swept away in violent succession, vv 1-28.
options.note: This is the repeated formula the narrator uses across all four reigns.

options.text: Each ruled from the city of Tirzah for a similar length of time.
options.note: True of some of them, but this is an incidental detail, not the chapter's central pattern.

options.text: Each was a direct descendant of King David.
options.note: Israel's northern kings were never of David's line; that line ruled only in Judah.

options.text: Each was a righteous king who sought the LORD faithfully.
options.note: The text says the opposite: each one did evil in the LORD's sight.

quiz.q: Why was Baasha's house judged and destroyed, according to Jehu's prophecy?

options.text: Because Baasha walked in the way of Jeroboam and caused Israel to sin, provoking the LORD to anger, vv 1-4, 7.
options.note: This is the explicit reason given in Jehu's prophecy.

options.text: Because Baasha had grown too powerful as a military leader.
options.note: A true detail about Baasha's background, but not the stated reason for judgment.

options.text: Because his son Elah personally wanted the throne sooner.
options.note: The judgment is against Baasha's own sins, not caused by Elah's ambition.

options.text: Because Baasha had honored the LORD too zealously.
options.note: The opposite is true; Baasha provoked the LORD by his sin, not his zeal for Him.

quiz.q: How long did Zimri reign, and how did his reign end?

options.text: Seven days; when Omri besieged Tirzah, Zimri burned the palace down upon himself, vv 15-18.
options.note: The text states this plainly and precisely.

options.text: Many years, until he was finally defeated by Omri's forces in old age.
options.note: The length of reign is wrong; it was only seven days, not years.

options.text: Zimri reigned peacefully and was never challenged.
options.note: His reign was immediately contested and ended in violent defeat.

options.text: Zimri was never actually made king over Israel.
options.note: The text explicitly says Zimri reigned in his place, verse 10.

quiz.q: What was distinctly worse about Ahab's sin compared to the kings before him, vv 30-33?

options.text: He not only walked in Jeroboam's sins but married Jezebel and built a temple, altar, and Asherah pole for Baal in Samaria itself.
options.note: This is the specific escalation the text names, provoking the LORD more than any king before him.

options.text: He was simply the first king to reign from the city of Samaria.
options.note: A true incidental detail from the previous verses about Omri, but it does not capture Ahab's distinct sin.

options.text: He merely continued his father's building projects without any new sin.
options.note: This understates the real escalation the text describes, the active installation of Baal worship.

options.text: He was actually more faithful to the LORD than his father Omri.
options.note: The text says the opposite: Ahab did evil more than all who were before him.

quiz.q: What is the significance of Hiel of Bethel rebuilding Jericho at the cost of his sons, verse 34?

options.text: It shows the LORD's word spoken generations earlier through Joshua being fulfilled exactly, proving His word never fails even after a long delay.
options.note: The verse explicitly ties this event to the word the LORD had spoken through Joshua son of Nun.

options.text: It shows that construction work in Ahab's reign was simply unusually dangerous.
options.note: A true incidental observation, but it misses the deliberate prophetic fulfillment the text highlights.

options.text: It shows Hiel was cursed for reasons unrelated to Joshua's ancient oath.
options.note: The text directly connects this event to Joshua's specific curse on Jericho's rebuilder.

options.text: It shows that the LORD's past words no longer carried any force by this time.
options.note: The opposite is true; the point is precisely that His word still carried full force.

questions: Baasha, Elah, Zimri, and Omri each grasped for the throne only to be swept away in turn. Where in your own life are you grasping for control or security in ways that will not last?

Generation after generation, Israel's kings simply repeated the sins of the one before them, treating it as normal. What patterns of sin have you inherited from family or culture without ever really questioning them?

Ahab did not merely tolerate other loyalties alongside the LORD; he built Baal a temple in the very city meant to serve God's people. Where have you made room for a rival at the center of your life rather than at its edges?

Centuries after Joshua's warning, the LORD's word about Jericho came true exactly as spoken, at great cost to Hiel's own family. What promise or warning of God's have you been slow to take seriously simply because time has passed?

In a chapter full of violent, short lived kings, the whole story quietly waits for a King who will not be swept away. How does knowing Christ's throne is secure change the way you face instability in your own life right now?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote gives two talents of silver as approximately 151 pounds or 68.4 kilograms, the price Omri paid for the hill of Samaria.
The BSB footnote points to Joshua 6:26, the curse Joshua pronounced on anyone who would rebuild Jericho, fulfilled here in the deaths of Hiel's sons.
'Worthless idols' translates a Hebrew term used elsewhere in Kings to dismiss false gods as vanities or nothings, underscoring the narrator's verdict on Baal worship.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Kings 16:1-34. No em dashes or en dashes appear in the source text for this chapter; none introduced elsewhere in this file.
